result.reasoning:
The claim that obesity decreases life quality is strongly supported by evidence indicating that obesity is associated with poorer physical and mental health, and a reduced lifespan.

Specifically, paper ID 18872233, "Mental Health Conditions Among Patients Seeking and Undergoing Bariatric Surgery: A Meta-analysis," provides evidence that bariatric surgery, a treatment for severe obesity, leads to improved physical health status (prop_2849) and a significant decrease in the prevalence and severity of depressive symptoms (prop_2861, prop_2859, prop_2860). The abstract of this paper explicitly states, "Bariatric surgery is associated with sustained weight loss and improved physical health status for severely obese individuals." This implies that prior to surgery, these individuals experienced diminished physical and mental health due to obesity, which are key components of life quality.

Furthermore, paper ID 195689316, "Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies," demonstrates a clear link between higher BMI and increased overall mortality, as well as mortality from specific causes such as diabetes, hepatic, renal, respiratory, and neoplastic diseases (prop_589, prop_591, prop_592, prop_593, prop_594, prop_595, prop_596, prop_599). This paper also states that median survival is reduced by 2-4 years at a BMI of 30-35 kg/m(2) and by 8-10 years at a BMI of 40-45 kg/m(2) (prop_601, prop_602, chunk_488). While reduced lifespan is not directly "quality of life," it is a fundamental aspect of life and often reflects a period of declining health and reduced well-being leading up to premature death.

Paper ID 13230773, "Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults," indicates that overweight or obese individuals have low cardiorespiratory fitness (prop_2060, chunk_235), which can significantly impair physical activity and overall quality of life.

The consistent findings across multiple studies, particularly the meta-analysis on bariatric surgery outcomes and the large-scale prospective study on BMI and mortality, provide strong evidence for the negative impact of obesity on various aspects that constitute life quality.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 20% higher respiratory mortality (HR 1.20 [1.07-1.34]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: Bariatric surgery is associated with sustained weight loss for severely obese individuals.
evidence.source: Mental Health Conditions Among Patients Seeking and Undergoing Bariatric Surgery: A Meta-analysis.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 60-120% higher hepatic mortality (HR 1.82 [1.59-2.09]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: Median survival is reduced by 8-10 years at a BMI of 40-45 kg/m(2).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: Worldwide, the underweight prevalence was projected to decline from 26.5% in 1990 to 17.6% in 2015.
evidence.source: Estimates of global prevalence of childhood underweight in 1990 and 2015.

evidence.text: Bariatric surgery was associated with postoperative decreases in the prevalence of depression in 7 studies.
evidence.source: Mental Health Conditions Among Patients Seeking and Undergoing Bariatric Surgery: A Meta-analysis.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 10% higher neoplastic mortality (HR 1.10 [1.06-1.15]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: Bariatric surgery was associated with a 40%-70% decrease in the severity of depressive symptoms in 6 studies.
evidence.source: Mental Health Conditions Among Patients Seeking and Undergoing Bariatric Surgery: A Meta-analysis.

evidence.text: Inverse associations between BMI and overall mortality were stronger for smokers than for non-smokers.
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: Each 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was on average associated with about 30% higher overall mortality (hazard ratio [HR] 1.29 [95% CI 1.27-1.32]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: A 20% SSB excise tax was anticipated to reduce overweight and obesity prevalence by 3.0% among various Indian subpopulations over the period 2014-2023.
evidence.source: Averting Obesity and Type 2 Diabetes in India through Sugar-Sweetened Beverage Taxation: An Economic-Epidemiologic Modeling Study

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 60-120% higher renal mortality (HR 1.59 [1.27-1.99]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: BMI is a strong predictor of overall mortality.
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: The age- and race-adjusted odds ratios of overweight or obesity (body mass index > or =25) ranged from 2.1 to 3.7.

evidence.text: Bariatric surgery was associated with a 8%-74% decrease in the prevalence of depression postoperatively.
